spans.content: Knowledge digitization and high-tech firm performance: A moderated mediation model incorporating business model innovation and entrepreneurial orientation
Wucheng Han a,1, Xiaoyu Li b,1, Weijie Zhu a,1, Ruoyu Lu a, Xu Zu c,*
School of Economics and Management, University of Electronic Science and Technology of China, Chengdu, China
<sup>b</sup> Business School, Renmin University of China, Beijing, China
<sup>c</sup> Business and Tourism School, Sichuan Agricultural University, Chengdu, China
ARTICLEINFO
Keywords:
Knowledge digitization
Business model innovation
Firm performance
Entrepreneurial orientation
High-tech firm
ABSTRACT

In today's rapidly evolving digital landscape, knowledge digitization, facilitated by advanced tools such as artificial intelligence, big data, blockchain, and cloud computing, has captured significant scholarly attention. To comprehensively explore the implications and outcomes of this knowledge digitization trend, our study delves into its impact on the performance of high-tech firms. Based on the knowledge-based view, we develop a moderated mediation model to examine the mediating role of business model innovation and the moderating effect of entrepreneurial orientation in the relationship between knowledge digitization and firm performance. To test our hypotheses, we employ the PLS-SEM method, analyzing data from a sample of 291 Chinese high-tech firms. Our findings indicate that knowledge digitization has a positive effect on firm performance, and both types of business model innovation—novel and efficient—partially mediate the positive relationship. Additionally, entrepreneurial orientation is found to strengthen the relationship between knowledge digitization and business model innovation. However, it does not significantly moderate the connection between knowledge digitization and firm performance. Our research offers actionable insights for strategic development in the digital era and makes a substantial contribution to the existing body of knowledge in this field.
1. Introduction

Traditional knowledge transmission, storage, and management rely on a single mode, thereby enterprises face significant challenges in rapidly acquiring information and effectively leveraging knowledge resources [1]. The digital revolution, empowered by the integration of artificial intelligence, big data, blockchain, and cloud computing, has ushered in a dynamic, accessible, and iteratively refined new era of knowledge. This evolution enables organizations to extend beyond traditional boundaries, fostering an environment conducive to enhanced digital knowledge management [2]. In this context, high-tech firms have emerged as pioneers strategically embracing knowledge digitization, thus demonstrating substantial operational improvements and success through the strategic adoption of digital knowledge practices [3].
Knowledge digitization leverages advanced information and

communication technologies—including digital systems, sensors, and machine vision—to convert comprehensive business, production, marketing, and technical knowledge into a digitally accessible format [1]. This transformation process not only facilitates the generation, storage, dissemination, and acquisition of knowledge but also fosters a deep integration of digital technology with knowledge resources [4]. Such integration significantly alters traditional knowledge forms and profoundly affects the carriers, distribution, and velocity of knowledge flow. Additionally, it facilitates the rapid absorption and transformation of diverse external knowledge. This promotes cross-departmental collaboration and fosters the development of new products, technologies, and services [5], signaling a potential paradigm shift in how firms leverage digital knowledge for a competitive advantage [6-8].
However, as enterprise businesses expand and the number of users increases, the amount of data that needs to be processed by enterprises
Technology in Society 77 (2024) 102536

experiences exponential growth. Ensuring data and knowledge accuracy and quality, as well as converting vast amounts of data and knowledge into competitive advantages and benefits for enterprises, pose significant challenges [9]. These challenges not only highlight the intricacies of implementing knowledge digitization but also raise a critical inquiry into its impact on firm performance. Knowledge digitization remains an emerging concept, with a paucity of empirical examinations into its relationship with firm performance. Hence, it is essential to initially ascertain the potential of knowledge digitization to bolster firm performance. Following this, a thorough investigation into the mechanisms through which knowledge digitization exerts its influence, alongside the exploration of boundary conditions that might moderate this relationship, becomes imperative. Such approach is critical for enriching theoretical frameworks in this field and bridging the substantial gap in our comprehension of how digitized knowledge assets can foster competitive advantage and propel organizational success [8].

Furthermore, scholars have recognized the significant role of enterprise innovation between knowledge digitization in promoting firm performance, with a focus on exploring its impact on technological and process innovation [1,10]. However, while technological innovation matters for market success, it alone may not suffice for ensuring long-time survival of firms. These firms need to innovate business models to unlock the full economic value of knowledge digitization [11]. Hence, business model innovation, referring to designed, novel, non-trivial changes to the key elements of a firm's business model and/or the architecture linking these elements [12], offers a vital framework. This framework aids in understanding how digitized knowledge assets can be transformed into new value propositions, revenue streams, and cost structures, potentially leading to significant performance improvements [13]. Knowledge digitization pervasively affects the way through which high-tech firms operate and support their business model, altering the very nature of products, processes, and strategies [14], and triggers transformations in the novel and efficient business models of high-tech firms [15]. In light of these considerations, this research seeks to bridge the gap in literature by systematically examining how knowledge digitization, through the lens of business model innovation, contributes to firm performance.

In addition, exploring the boundary conditions under which knowledge digitization impacts firm performance is pivotal for a nuanced understanding of this relationship. To effectively translate digital knowledge into enterprise performance, enterprises must promptly transform this knowledge into products, technologies, and services that meet customer demands and secure first-mover competitive advantages [16]. Entrepreneurial orientation is defined as the processes, practices, and decision-making activities that facilitate new market entries [17]. It emphasizes a firm's inclination towards innovation, continuous revision of products and services, exploitation of niche market opportunities, and willingness to undertake risks associated with uncertain projects [18]. Specifically, firms with a strong entrepreneurial orientation are better positioned to navigate the complexities of digital markets, adapt to rapid technological changes, and implement innovative business models that exploit digitized knowledge [19]. The proactiveness, risk-taking, and innovativeness of entrepreneurial orientation can facilitate the identification and exploitation of new value propositions, customer segments, and revenue streams enabled by digital transformation [14], thereby augmenting the relationship between knowledge digitization and firm performance. By examining entrepreneurial orientation as a moderating factor, this study aims to delineate the conditions under which knowledge digitization most effectively contributes to firm performance through business model innovation.

To solve the theoretical gaps above, we develop a theoretical framework to explore the mechanisms and boundary conditions under which knowledge digitization affects firm performance. We focus our empirical investigation on high-tech firms. These firms are characterized by their operation in a dynamic business environment, with rapid technological advancement, complexity, and heightened uncertainty [11].

The diversity and innovative nature of their knowledge management practices offer insights into distinct knowledge digitization strategies. Given their pivotal role in driving the digital economy and their advanced utilization of digital infrastructure, high-tech firms are well-suited for this line of research [3]. We apply partial least squares structural equation modeling (PLS-SEM) to analyze the data collected from 291 Chinese high-tech firms.

This study offers significant theoretical and practical contributions to the field of knowledge digitization. First, by delving into this burgeoning field in exploring the impact of knowledge digitization on firm performance, the study not only enriches the existing discourse but also lays the groundwork for subsequent theoretical and empirical explorations [1]. Second, this research addresses the emerging theoretical challenges in this field by verifying the mediating effect of business model innovation on the relationship between knowledge digitization and firm performance. This contribution is particularly valuable given the scarcity of empirical studies in this nascent area, offering new insights into the mechanisms that underlie these relationships [20]. Third, by identifying the moderating role of entrepreneurial orientation on the effects of knowledge digitization, this study broadens our understanding of entrepreneurial orientation as an important facilitator in a digital context [21,22].

The structure of this study is organized as follows: the second section delineates the theoretical foundation and the development of hypotheses; the third section outlines the research methodology, encompassing data collection and sampling, measurement techniques, and the approach to data analysis. The fourth section details the empirical results, including analyses of common method bias, the measurement and structural models, as well as the results of hypothesis testing. The final section, the discussion, synthesizes the study's findings, articulates its theoretical and practical implications, and discusses limitations and directions for future research.
2. Theoretical foundation and hypotheses development
2.1. Knowledge-based view

The knowledge-based view posits that knowledge serves as a strategic resource within organizations, driving innovation, competitive advantage, and value creation [23,24]. Consistent with the knowledge-based view, firms optimize their knowledge management processes to enhance knowledge diversity and heterogeneity, while also strengthening stakeholder interactions to broaden information and knowledge sources [25]. Additionally, firms develop dynamic capabilities to continuously absorb, integrate, and utilize knowledge. This proficiency directly and significantly influences knowledge-related performance [26].

In the digital era, the integration of digital technology and knowledge management has revolutionized the process and effectiveness of knowledge management [27]. Knowledge digitization facilitates automatic and extensive knowledge search, conversion of tacit knowledge into explicit digitally stored information, and enables value creation from knowledge [1]. Grounded in the principles of the knowledge base view, this process enhances a company's knowledge base and subsequently improves its performance [28]. Digital knowledge management platforms transcend physical boundaries, facilitating extensive knowledge storage, search, sharing, and utilization. Digital technology fosters value co-creation through global knowledge access, collaboration, and leveraging of external knowledge sources [29]. Moreover, digital technologies empower data-driven decision-making by harnessing real-time, structured, and unstructured data for opportunity identification and enhanced decision-making processes [30].

While knowledge digitization offers opportunities for operational enhancement, it presents notable theoretical and practical challenges to the knowledge-based view. One primary concern is the surge in digitized knowledge, complicating the integration and analysis processes.
W. Han et al.
Technology in Society 77 (2024) 102536

Furthermore, the absence of a robust validity assessment in digitization could potentially diminish the efficacy of knowledge management practices [31]. Another challenge is the digitization process's heavy dependence on explicit knowledge, risking the diminution of the depth inherent in human-centered explicit knowledge. This dependence might also attenuate the quality of human-computer interaction within organizational setups [32]. Lastly, concerns around data security and privacy emerge with knowledge digitization. Such concerns pose restrictions on knowledge storage and dissemination, endangering the distinctiveness and confidentiality of an organization's knowledge assets [33,34].

Notwithstanding the aforementioned challenges, the intrinsic strategic value of digitized knowledge cannot be overlooked. Contemporary firms are progressively integrating knowledge digitization into their strategic frameworks, recognizing its potential to be harnessed as a significant asset in the digital era [35]. Given the paramount importance of this dynamic and its intricate nature, it is imperative to discern the direct, indirect and contingent effects of knowledge digitization on firm performance [27].
2.2. Knowledge digitization and firm performance

Knowledge digitization involves transforming organizational knowledge across various domains, including business, production, marketing, and technical aspects, into a format that is amenable to storage, computation, and analysis through technologies such as information and communication technologies (ICT), information systems, sensors, machine vision, and digital technologies [36]. This process enables organizations to utilize digital tools and platforms for efficient storage, retrieval, and analysis of knowledge resources, which has three distinct characteristics: codability, convergence, and generativity [1]. Codability refers to an organization's capacity to process data through digital technologies, facilitating the decoupling of information from specific technical devices, storage media, and transmission formats. This capability allows for the encoding and storage of information in structured database formats, enhancing accessibility and usability [37]. Convergence implies that the boundaries between industries, organizations, departments, and even products become less significant, and knowledge gradually becomes transparent and centralized [5]. Generativity refers to the dynamic, self-referential, scalable, and editable nature of digital technologies, enabling real-time tracking and analysis of knowledge. This leads to the availability of the most up-to-date and effective knowledge, thereby facilitating continuous improvement and enabling adjustments in innovative activities [30].

We propose that knowledge digitization promotes firm performance for the following reasons. First, the codability of knowledge digitization enables the compilation and storage of knowledge in databases, facilitating its integration with internal knowledge. This integration allows for efficient access and utilization by individuals within the organization, which increases productivity [36]. Furthermore, such codification substantially reduces expenses associated with physical storage and labor-intensive retrieval processes [38]. By improving access and simplifying the retrieval of knowledge, this transformative strategy not only boosts organizational efficiency but also results in significant cost reductions, thereby enhancing overall performance [39].

Second, convergence, by digitally linking suppliers, manufacturers, and distributors, streamlines supply chain integration and collaboration. This seamless information flow and coordination among different stakeholders optimize supply chain operations, leading to enhanced overall operational efficiency [40]. Additionally, convergence encourages collaboration across departments, which fosters the development of new products, technologies, and services, thereby creating sustainable competitive advantages [41].

Third, generativity, a feature of digital technologies, facilitates the creation of new knowledge and skills. The integration of new and existing knowledge enriches the firm's knowledge base, increasing the diversity, depth, and breadth of knowledge [42]. Digital platforms,

supported by real-time data and analytical tools, improve the intelligence and timeliness of knowledge acquisition [43]. The adaptability and flexibility provided by digital technologies allow firms to effectively respond to external environmental changes, thereby enhancing firm performance [6,44]. Hence, we propose the following hypothesis.
Hypothesis 1. Knowledge digitization has a positive effect on firm performance.
2.3. Knowledge digitization, business model innovation and firm performance

Bucherer et al. [45] defined business model innovation as a deliberate process of changing the core elements and business logic of a company, emphasizing designed, novel, non-trivial changes to the key elements of a firm's business model and/or the architecture linking these elements [12]. Zott and Amit [15] categorized business model innovation into two distinct types: novel business model innovation, which focuses on introducing innovative transaction content and methodologies, and efficient business model innovation, aimed at reducing transaction costs and enhancing efficiency. Novel business model innovation seeks to redefine economic transactions with partners, introducing new methods or value propositions [46,47], whereas efficient business model innovation targets transaction cost minimization to boost efficiency [48,49].

Knowledge digitization facilitates novel business model innovation. First, codability enables organizations to integrate internal and external knowledge resources by leveraging digital technologies, thereby developing unique value propositions and differentiating market strategies [50]. Digitized knowledge resources support the iterative development and refinement of innovative business models. This support helps organizations to identify and address potential risks and uncertainties, thereby increasing the likelihood of successful innovation [16]. Second, convergence facilitates the integration of diverse technologies, services, and platforms, fostering the formation of collaborative ecosystems and value networks that drive business model innovation [51]. Through digital platforms and connectivity, organizations can access complementary knowledge and capabilities, incorporate new technological advancements into their business models, and develop novel value propositions and revenue streams [52]. Third, generativity enables real-time tracking, analysis, and utilization of knowledge, providing organizations with timely insights and adaptability [30,53]. Through relentless surveillance and nuanced analysis of market trajectories, consumer propensities, and burgeoning technologies, enterprises can assimilate contemporary techniques, market trends, and industry best practices to iteratively refine their business paradigms, ensuring alignment with the fluid market demands and capitalization on emergent opportunities [90,91].

Knowledge digitization also benefits efficient business model. To begin with, codability allows organizations to automate and optimize processes by digitizing and encoding knowledge, resulting in reduced costs, increased productivity, and higher levels of process efficiency [47]. Digitized knowledge resources provide data-driven insights and decision support capabilities, beneficial for improving efficiency in business operations [54]. Additionally, convergence facilitates the integration and collaboration within supply chains and value chains [55], enabling seamless information flow and coordination among different stakeholders. This leads to optimized supply chain operations, streamlined transactions, resource sharing, and collaborative innovation, thereby enhancing overall operational efficiency [8,13]. Moreover, generativity enables organizations to identify inefficiencies, adapt quickly to changing market conditions and customer demands, and implement lean practices by leveraging real-time data and feedback, ultimately achieving operational excellence [40]. Based on the above analysis, we propose the following hypothesis.
Hypothesis 2. Knowledge digitization has a positive effect on (a)
W. Han et al.
Technology in Society 77 (2024) 102536
novel business model innovation and (b) efficient business model innovation.

Business model innovation plays a crucial role in creating value, gaining a competitive advantage, promoting firm performance, and driving entrepreneurial growth [16]. First, novel business model innovation focuses on exploring and satisfying market and consumer needs by introducing new products or services and leveraging new market spaces to access potential consumers, partners, and suppliers [56]. By effectively allocating resources and capturing value-added in the original market, firms can achieve wealth and revenue acquisition across organizational boundaries [57]. Furthermore, novel business model innovation enables firms to develop new transaction and incentive models, enhancing customer experiences, transaction methods, and value creation processes, thereby upgrading the value of potential resources in existing markets and achieving higher performance levels [58]. Empirical studies and theoretical foundations in economics and management support the notion that novel business model innovation improves firm performance [19].

Second, efficient business model innovation significantly enhances firm performance for two primary reasons. Initially, it strengthens the connections between firms and various stakeholders. A stronger link between the firm and its partners leads to more and larger deals, while increased interdependence and reliance on partners raise the switching costs, granting the firm assertive negotiation and bargaining power [59]. Moreover, efficient business models facilitate information exchange among participants, accelerating the speed of information sharing and reducing information asymmetry between partners. This reduces the distortion of partners' product and demand information and allows companies to aggregate market demand more effectively, resulting in reduced transaction costs and improved firm performance [47]. We thus propose the following hypothesis.
Hypothesis 3. Novel business model innovation (a) and efficient business model innovation (b) have a positive impact on firm performance.

Through the development of a business model aligned with knowledge digitization, firms can strategically reconfigure or create new environments that better suit their survival, enhancing their adaptability to market dynamics and resilience to innovation risks [60,61]. The characteristics of codability, convergence, and generativity inherent in knowledge digitization play a significant role in promoting business model innovation, thereby impacting firm performance. For novel business model innovation, codability enables firms to efficiently gather and analyze customer data, accurately pinpoint customer needs, and introduce differentiated products and services, thereby enhancing firm performance [49]. Convergence leverages digital technology to cross-fertilize knowledge from diverse domains, facilitating rapid strategic adjustments and the formation of novel business models that are responsive to changing market environments and maintain competitive edges [60]. Furthermore, generativity augments enterprises' capacity to generate novel knowledge and information through digital technology, driving innovation in product development, design, and manufacturing, and leading to the creation of competitive new products and services that bolster corporate performance [62].

To foster efficient business model innovation, codability enables enterprises to translate intricate business processes and knowledge into executable digital codes, significantly minimizing manual intervention and mitigating errors. By leveraging encoded data and processes, organizations can gain a more precise understanding of resource allocation and achieve optimal resource utilization. These advancements have significantly augmented resource utilization efficiency and work productivity, ultimately enhancing enterprise performance [36]. Convergence facilitates knowledge flow and collaborative efforts within enterprises, enabling organizations to break down information silos, improve cross-departmental collaboration, and expedite the

responsiveness and adaptability of their business models. This integration reduces costs and bolsters corporate performance [5]. Generativity urges enterprises to continually explore and adopt novel digital tools and methods, such as the development of AI tools and automated analysis instruments. These advancements assist employees in efficiently executing tasks, elevating the pace of innovation and operational efficiency, and ultimately enhancing operational performance [28]. Therefore, this study posits that both novel and efficient business model innovations serve as mediators between knowledge digitization and firm performance. Consequently, the following hypothesis is proposed.
Hypothesis 4. Novel business model innovation (a) and efficient business model innovation (b) mediate the relationship between knowledge digitization and firm performance.
2.4. The moderating role of entrepreneurial orientation

Entrepreneurial orientation, as proposed by Covin & Slevin [63], refers to a firm's overall orientation towards competition in the industry, characterized by a willingness to take risks and embrace innovation and change as means to gain a competitive advantage. The core components of entrepreneurial orientation are innovativeness, proactiveness, and risk-taking. Innovativeness entails actively pursuing growth opportunities and focusing on product innovation and technological advancements in the firm's development process [64]. Proactiveness emphasizes a firm's proactive stance in anticipating market needs within its industry segment, enabling them to quickly identify and exploit market opportunities to gain a first-mover advantage [17]. Risk-taking underscores a firm's willingness to engage in high-risk and high-cost projects, allocate resources to uncertain endeavors, and explore the unknown [65,66].

Firms that embrace entrepreneurial orientation in a dynamic competitive environment are more likely to adapt their business practices [16]. Innovativeness plays a pivotal role in stimulating innovation consciousness and behavior within firms, guiding them to pursue opportunities, create new value, and seek innovative solutions. Entrepreneurial-oriented firms are more inclined to proactively leverage the opportunities presented by knowledge digitization to engage in innovative activities, such as acquiring new products, technologies, and ideas, and exploring novel business model innovations [22]. Proactiveness emphasizes the ability to rapidly respond to changing environments and market demands. In the context of knowledge digitization, entrepreneurial-oriented firms can proactively identify gap markets and potential needs, actively seek market opportunities, and utilize digital tools and platforms to flexibly acquire, integrate, and apply knowledge resources. This enables them to transform new business opportunities and continuously optimize operational efficiency into tangible performance outcomes [67]. Risk-taking drives firms to engage in collaborative innovation and resource integration with external partners, enabling them to access additional knowledge and technological resources while mitigating operational risks. Entrepreneurial-oriented firms can leverage digital platforms to share knowledge and engage in collaborative innovation with partners, as well as invest resources in high-risk yet promising projects [53,68]. Entrepreneurial-oriented firms can leverage digital platforms to share knowledge and engage in collaborative innovation with partners, as well as invest resources in high-risk yet promising projects. Based on the above discussion, this paper proposes the following hypothesis.

Hypothesis 5. Entrepreneurial orientation positively moderates the relationships between knowledge digitization and (a) novel business model innovation, (b) efficient business model innovation, (c) firm performance, such that the relationships are stronger with a higher level of entrepreneurial orientation.

Hypothesis 6. Entrepreneurial orientation positively moderates the indirect effect of (a) novel business model innovation and (b) efficient business model innovation between knowledge digitization and firm
W. Han et al.
Technology in Society 77 (2024) 102536
performance, such that the indirect relationships are stronger with a higher level of entrepreneurial orientation.
The conceptual framework underpinning our inquiry is illustrated in Fig. 1.
3. Method
3.1. Data collection and sample

This study seeks to examine the relationships among knowledge digitization, business model innovation, firm performance, and entrepreneurial orientation within the context of high-tech firms. The rationale for selecting Chinese high-tech firms as the focus of this investigation stems from multiple factors. Primarily, China's rapid rise in the digital economy, establishing it as one of the global leaders, underscores the pertinence of examining these relationships within its high-tech sector. McKinsey's research highlights the challenge of digitization faced by Chinese firms also established a compelling case for the geographical focus of this study [69]. Moreover, high-tech firms are inherently at the forefront of digital innovation and transformation. Their active engagement with advanced technologies makes them as ideal subjects for exploring the nuances of knowledge digitization and its effects on business model innovation and firm performance. The insights gained from this study are expected to mirror the cutting-edge trends and practices in the field [5]. Lastly, the inherent competitive dynamism and the fast-paced evolution characteristic of high-tech industries necessitate a multifaceted and innovative approach to knowledge management. This aspect further validates the selection of high-tech firms as a fertile ground for exploring varied strategies of knowledge digitization and their consequential effects on organizational innovation and performance [3,11].

We developed a survey targeting senior executives of high-tech firms located in the Chengdu Hi-Tech Industrial Development Zone, a nationally recognized economic development area in China that hosts a diverse spectrum of advanced technology parks across sectors such as information technology, biomedicine, software development, aerospace, new materials, and the broader digital economy. This zone, serving as a hub for over 3000 certified high-tech enterprises, represents a vibrant innovation ecosystem. The certification criteria for these enterprises emphasize the area's dedication to technological advancement, mandating that firms have been operational for more than one year, hold substantial intellectual property rights, and significantly engage in high-tech fields. A requirement that research and development personnel make up at least
of the total workforce further highlights these firms' commitment to innovation.

The survey was structured into two sections. The first section sought basic demographic information about the respondents, including gender, age, and education level, alongside organizational details such as the firm's age, employee count, and the implementation of digital technology in knowledge management. The second section was designed to assess key constructs of interest: knowledge digitization,
innovative business model implementation, entrepreneurial orientation, and firm performance.

In April 2023, with local government support, we reached out to executives from over 500 recognized high-tech companies, dispatching a total of 521 questionnaires via email. After filtering out incomplete or non-qualifying responses, we secured a valid sample of 291 questionnaires, equating to a response rate of approximately
. Descriptive statistics of the respondents and their firms are summarized in Table 1, offering a comprehensive overview of the sample's characteristics.
3.2. Measurement

We adapted measurement items for all variables from previous studies. Measurement items are attached in the appendix. All variables in this study were assessed using a five-point Likert scale, ranging from 1 (strongly disagree) to 5 (strongly agree). To measure knowledge

digitization, we employed the seven-item scale developed by Cheng et al. [1]. Business model innovation, differentiated into novel and efficient categories [15], was evaluated through four items each, based on the classification and measurement scale proposed by Zott and Amit [47]. Firm performance was measured by four items, referencing the empirical work of Klassen and McLaughlin [70] and Collins and Smith [71]. Entrepreneurial orientation was quantified using a five-item scale, integrating the methodologies of Jambulingam et al. [72] and Han et al. [16]. In this study, control variables are categorized into two primary components: the first pertains to the gender, age, and education level of the respondents; the second component includes questions about the length of establishment of their company, the number of employees, the industry, and the type of digital technology utilized.
3.3. Data analysis

For data analysis and hypothesis testing in this study, we adopted Partial Least Squares Structural Equation Modeling (PLS-SEM), a method recognized for its flexibility and robustness within management research [73,74]. PLS-SEM is particularly suited for complex structural models, such as the one in this study, which incorporates five variables [74]. This method is also valuable for examining mediating and moderating effects, offering insights into the underlying dynamics of our model [75]. Moreover, PLS-SEM is applicable to a wide range of sample sizes, accommodating both large and small sample studies [76]. To further validate the mediating effects within our model, we employed a bootstrap method involving 5000 sub-samples, ensuring the reliability and stability of our findings. Smart PLS 4 software facilitated the path model analysis. Following the guidelines set forth by Henseler et al. [77], we structured the interpretation of the PLS-SEM findings into two distinct phases: first, we assessed the measurement model to confirm the precise measurement and accurate reflection of theoretical concepts by each construct; subsequently, we evaluated the structural model to examine the validity of our research hypotheses.
4. Results
4.1. Common method bias

To further enhance data reliability and minimize common method bias, the survey was designed for anonymous completion and the questions were randomized to prevent any order effects [78]. To examine potential multicollinearity, we tested for variance inflation factor (VIF), with values exceeding 5 indicating possible concerns [76]. The results showed all VIF scores were below this threshold, suggesting no multicollinearity. Additionally, we applied the unmeasured latent common method factor (ULCMF) technique, revealing that common method variance was minimal [79]. Confirmatory factor analysis supported a five-factor model
\mathrm{CFI} = 0.950
\mathrm{TLI} = 0.943
\mathrm{RMSEA} = 0.030)
, which was superior to a one-factor model
\Delta df = 10
and showed only slight differences compared to the ULCMF model, indicating the robustness of our measurements against common method bias

\Delta \mathrm{TLI} = 0.006
, and
\Delta \mathrm{RMSEA} = 0.003)
. The results of the common method bias analysis are detailed in Table 2.
Table 2 Common method bias analysis.
4.2. Measurement model

The model structure of this study was tested for reliability and validity, as shown in Table 3. First, all items in the five variables had factor loadings greater than 0.7, which supports the reliability of the indicators [74]. Second, the Cronbach's alpha
(\alpha)

of all variables was higher than 0.8, which is consistent with the interval that should be higher than 0.7. Moreover, the composite reliability (CR) values were higher than 0.8, which is greater than the minimum standard criterion of 0.5, indicating that the construct reliability of the scale is excellent [76]. Third, the average variance extracted (AVE) of all variables is greater than 0.6, indicating that the indicator variable can effectively reflect its latent variable, which has good reliability and validity, which supports the convergent validity of the scale construct measure [77].

Finally, the paper examines the discriminant validity between the variables, as shown in Table 4. The AVE of each construct should be compared with the squared inter-structural correlation (as a measure of shared variance) for that same construct and with all other reflectivity measures in the structural model. In addition, the shared variance of all model configurations should not be greater than their AVE [76]. The heterotrait-monotrait (HTMT) ratios of correlations are all below the threshold of 0.85 [80], indicating the discriminant validity of the scale.

), indicating the high predictive accuracy of the constructed structural model [73].

performance

, thus H1 is supported. The path coefficient of knowledge digitization on novel business model innovation is 0.48 (t = 9.874), indicating that knowledge digitization positively influences novel business model innovation, supporting H2a. The path coefficient of knowledge digitization on efficient business model innovation is 0.45, and the t-value is 7.349, which indicates that knowledge digitization positively affects efficient business model innovation, and H2b is supported. The path coefficient of novel business model innovation on firm performance is 0.331, and the t-value is 3.868, providing support for H3a. The path coefficient of efficient business model innovation on firm performance is 0.169 (t = 1.99, p < 0.05), indicating that efficient business model innovation positively affects firm performance, thus confirms H3b.

The mediation effect of novel business model innovation and efficient business model innovation between knowledge digitization and firm performance was examined using a bootstrap approach based on 5000 sub-samples. The results showed that the path coefficient of novel business model innovation between knowledge digitization and firm

performance is 0.159 (t = 3.546). The VAF value is 0.641 (greater than 0.2, less than 0.8), indicating that novel business model innovation partially mediates between knowledge digitization and firm performance [56]. Thus, H4a is confirmed. The path coefficient of efficient business model innovation between knowledge digitization and firm performance was 0.76, the t-value was 1.961, p < 0.05, and the VAF value was 0.461 (greater than 0.2 and less than 0.8). The result aligns with H4b, which indicates that efficient business model innovation partially mediates between knowledge digitization and firm performance [56].
The results of moderating effect testing show that entrepreneurial orientation positively moderates the relationship between knowledge digitization and novel business model innovation (
), efficient business model innovation (

), supporting H5a and H5b. However, the relationship between entrepreneurial orientation in knowledge digitization and firm performance is 0.011 with a t-value of 0.3, thus of H5c is not supported according to the result. Fig. 3 demonstrates the moderating effect of entrepreneurial orientation in the relationship between knowledge digitization and novel business model innovation, efficient business model innovation.

Utilizing bootstrapping for confidence interval (CI) estimation, we analyzed the conditional influence of entrepreneurial orientation on the indirect effect of business model innovation between knowledge digitization and firm performance [92]. The index for the first moderated mediating path is 0.055 (95% CI = [0.009, 0.109]), signifying a significant moderated mediation effect, wherein entrepreneurial orientation reinforces the indirect connection between knowledge digitization and firm performance through novel business model innovation. Similarly,

the index for the second moderated mediating path is 0.034 (95% CI = [0.002, 0.069]), also indicating a significant moderated mediation effect, where entrepreneurial orientation strengthens the indirect relationship between knowledge digitization and firm performance through efficient business model innovation, H6a and H6b are supported.
5. Discussion

This research uncovers several key findings. Initially, there is a discernible positive correlation between knowledge digitization and firm performance. Furthermore, the study verifies, through the lens of Zott & Amit's [15] classification, that both innovative and efficient forms of business model innovation act as partial mediators in the relationship between knowledge digitization and firm performance. Additionally, it is evidenced that entrepreneurial orientation bolsters the effects of knowledge digitization and business model innovation.

However, contrary to the suggestions of perspective of Niemand et al. [83], our findings indicate that the moderating role of entrepreneurial orientation in the link between knowledge digitization and firm performance is not significant. This divergence can be ascribed to several factors. First, the volume and diversity of knowledge produced through digitization may complicate the processes of knowledge creation, absorption, and integration [9], potentially leading to a "diversity dilemma" that weakens the influence of entrepreneurial orientation [28]. Second, the risk-taking associated with an entrepreneurial approach to new products, technologies, and services may introduce uncertainties that mitigate the advantages of knowledge digitization for firm performance [17].
5.1. Implications for theory

First, as one of the pioneering empirical examinations in the field of knowledge digitization, this study explores its impact on firm performance, enriching the discourse in this burgeoning field and establishing groundwork for subsequent theoretical and empirical explorations. Recognizing the acknowledged importance of knowledge digitization in the creation and management of digital knowledge through technological means [1], the research seeks to address the insufficiently explored direct impact of knowledge digitization on firm performance—an essential metric for gauging the success of business strategies within management studies. The investigation delves into how and when knowledge digitization affects firm performance, thus enhancing our understanding of knowledge management amid digital transformation [27]. Additionally, by developing and testing a hypothesized model, it extends the application of the knowledge-based view to the high-tech industry context, offering a valuable addition to current studies [35].
Second, this study cracked the black box of the relationship of business model innovation between knowledge digitization and firm

performance, and expanded the research depth on how enterprises can obtain competitive advantage through innovation based on knowledge digitization. Previous scholars have emphasized the significance of technological innovation and process innovation in the relationship between knowledge digitization and firm performance [1,10]. Nevertheless, research on business model innovation remains underrepresented. This study draws on the categorization of business model innovation by Zott and Amit [15], exploring the effects of knowledge digitization on both novel and efficient business model innovations, and validates their mediating roles between knowledge digitization and firm performance. This deepens the breadth of research on knowledge digitization's impact on corporate innovation, providing theoretical support for how knowledge digitization drives business model innovation, thereby enhancing firm competitive advantage [20].

Third, this study introduces entrepreneurial orientation as a boundary condition, highlighting the moderating effect of entrepreneurial orientation between knowledge digitization and business model innovation, thereby broadening the understanding of entrepreneurial orientation in a digital context [21]. Our findings confirm that entrepreneurial orientation amplifies the relationship between knowledge digitization and both types of business model innovation—efficient and novel. The moderated mediation model illustrates that entrepreneurial orientation strengthened the facilitating effect of knowledge digitization on firm performance through business model innovation. This exploration into the intricate dynamics between knowledge digitization and firm performance enriches our comprehension of the interaction between knowledge management and entrepreneurial orientation [84]. The results contribute to theoretical advancement in the domain, aligning with and expanding upon seminal works in the field of entrepreneurship [17], by delineating the conditions under which entrepreneurial orientation can serve as a catalyst in the digital knowledge to performance conversion process.
5.2. Implications for practice

First, in the contemporary knowledge economy, enterprises must understand the transformative potential of knowledge digitization. This process is key to optimizing resource allocation, enhancing decision-making, fostering product innovation, and increasing market competitiveness[85]. For high-tech firms, capitalizing on these benefits requires a strategic framework that positions knowledge as a vital asset. Such a framework should also recognize the central role of digitization in driving organizational growth. Investing in robust digital infrastructure is critical. High-tech firms should employ advanced technologies like cloud computing, big data analytics, and artificial intelligence. These tools can streamline the acquisition, integration, analysis, and application of knowledge [86]. Furthermore, as the volume and complexity of digital knowledge grow, establishing comprehensive data security and
W. Han et al.
Technology in Society 77 (2024) 102536

privacy systems is imperative. These systems ensure the confidentiality, integrity, and accessibility of corporate information. To maintain the effectiveness of knowledge digitization, enterprises should regularly evaluate their initiatives. This involves reviewing the alignment between digitization strategies and business objectives, assessing the success of digital projects, and making adjustments to improve strategy execution.

Second, in the realm of high-tech firms, the trajectory of sustainable development is increasingly contingent upon the innovative evolution of business models, a process that is significantly enhanced by the strategic application of knowledge digitization. The imperative for business model innovation becomes a cornerstone for securing a competitive advantage, thereby facilitating sustainable development and the maximization of value within enterprises. Through knowledge digitization, firms are equipped to attain a nuanced comprehension of market dynamics and consumer preferences, thereby enabling the refinement and differentiation of their business models [87]. For example, the adoption of digital technologies fosters the creation of agile supply chains that support personalized customization and expedited delivery mechanisms. Moreover, the integration of big data analytics and artificial intelligence technologies is crucial in refining marketing strategies and delivering personalized services. This tailored approach significantly enhances customer engagement and fosters loyalty [86]. In the process of implementing these digitization strategies, it is essential for high-tech firms to cultivate an organizational culture that champions knowledge sharing and innovation. Promoting active engagement in knowledge exchange and innovation-driven activities among employees is critical for dismantling traditional knowledge silos and enhancing the dissemination and enrichment of knowledge across the organization.

Third, integrating digital knowledge with an entrepreneurial orientation is pivotal for high-tech firms in the identification of novel opportunities, the acceleration of digital transformation, and the attainment of a competitive edge. Initially, it is imperative for these firms to cultivate an organizational culture that champions entrepreneurial orientation, characterized by openness, inclusiveness, and collaboration. Such a culture encourages employees to challenge conventional paradigms, innovate, and commit to the realization of new ideas [88]. Moreover, assembling a multidisciplinary team that amalgamates diverse expertise and skills fosters the generation of innovative and practical solutions. This interdisciplinary collaboration not only promotes the integration of varied knowledge domains but also bolsters team cohesion and creativity. Furthermore, for sustained innovation and competitive agility, firms must engage in ongoing evaluation and adaptation of their innovation strategies. The systematic collection and analysis of data pertaining to market feedback, customer needs, and technological trends enable firms to identify emergent innovation opportunities promptly and recalibrate their strategic objectives accordingly. Engaging in external partnerships with technology startups, academic institutions, and industry specialists further augments access to novel knowledge resources and insights into cutting-edge digital trends.
5.3. Limitations and future directions

This study recognizes several limitations that future inquiries should address. First, reliance on self-reported questionnaires may lead to common method bias, potentially affecting the results' reliability. Future research could mitigate this by employing a mixed-methods approach, incorporating both quantitative and qualitative data from diverse sources, including objective performance metrics and evaluations by independent observers. Such a methodology would enrich the analysis and bolster the study's validity.

Second, the research sample was confined to Chinese high-tech firms, potentially constraining the generalizability of our findings across different industries or geographic areas. Future studies should endeavor to include a broader array of participants from varied sectors and global regions to ensure the wider applicability and relevance of the results.

Third, our conceptual framework does not encompass the influence of multi-level organizational and external environmental variables on the knowledge digitization process [89]. Subsequent research should explore the multifaceted mechanisms and contextual factors that modulate the impact of knowledge digitization on firm performance. This includes examining the role of leadership traits, organizational culture, workforce skills, and the broader industry and market conditions.

Knowledge digitizationKD1Our company has the ability to release knowledge using digital technology[1]
KD2Our company has the ability to achieve network management using digital technology
KD3Our company has the information technology level of encoding and storing knowledge
KD4Our company has utilized digital technology to enhance knowledge collaboration
KD5Our company has utilized digital technologies to enhance the boundaries of knowledge integration and assimilation
KD6Our company has utilized digital technology to facilitate user participation in knowledge generation
KD7Our company has utilized digital technology to achieve continuous trial and error and iteration of knowledge
Novel business model innovationNBM1The business model of the corporation can reintegrate the output services and products[47]
NBM2The corporation would use innovative incentive measures to increase the enthusiasm of business model participants
NBM3The business model of the corporation has the most significant number of products or the most styled participants in history
NBM4The focal firm has continuously introduced innovations in its business model
Efficient business model innovationEBM1The business model of the corporation can avoid errors in the transaction process as much as possible[47]
EBM2The business model of the corporation is highly applicable, able to handle large-scale or small-scale transaction activities
EBM3The business model of the corporation can summarize the information, participants and services of the corporation in a larger area
EBM4Overall, our business model can enable us to attain faster transaction efficiency
Firm performanceFP1Compared with competitors in the industry, the corporation has better profitability[70, 71]
FP2Compared with competitors in the industry, the corporation has higher profit margins
FP3Compared with competitors in the industry, the corporation has a higher market share
FP4Compared with competitors in the industry, the corporation has a higher sales growth rate
Entrepreneurial orientationEO1The corporation encouraged and introduced innovative ideas, products and services[16, 72]
EO2The corporate leaders emphasized scientific research, technology leadership and innovation
EO3The corporation strongly supported high-risk projects
EO4The executives have introduced new ideas and products ahead of others
EO5Facing competitors, the corporation took the lead in introducing new products, services, management and operation technologies
